coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots decorated rutile TiO2 nanoflowers for water splitting application
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2017 Science Press and Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier B.V. and Science Press. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Science Press and Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier B.V. and Science Press.
coredata.dc:creator: Bayat, A.
Saievar-Iranizad, E.
coredata.dc:description: Flower like rutile TiO2 films were decorated with green-photoluminescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and photovoltaic properties were investigated for water splitting application. Rutile TiO2 nanoflowers (NFs) and GQDs (average width of ∼12 nm) synthesized separately using a hydrothermal method and TiO2 NFs were decorated with various amounts of GQDs solution (x = 5, 10, 15 and 20 µL) by spin coating. Optical characterization reveals that GQDs are highly luminescent and absorb UV and visible light photons with wavelengths up to 700 nm. GQDs-x/TiO2 electrode shows a photocurrent enhancement of ∼ 95% compared to pristine TiO2 NFs for the optimum sample (x = 15 µL) at an applied potential of P = 0 V using 1 M Na2SO4 solution as electrolyte.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
TiO2 nanoflowers
Water splitting
Photoanode

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Decorating of TiO2 NFs with GQDs
Generation mechanism of H2 and O2 molecules

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2017 Science Press and Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier B.V. and Science Press. All rights reserved.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a, b) Top-view SEM images of the TiO2 NFs grown on the FTO substrate at different scales (inset shows the cross section SEM image), (c) TEM image of the GQDs and (d) TEM image of the GQDs decorated TiO2 NFs.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of glucose and GQDs solutions and the inset shows PL spectra of GQDs solution excited at the wavelengths from 450 to 520 nm and photographs show GQDs solution under daylight and UV illumination, (b) FTIR spectrum of the GQDs showing functional groups and (c) Raman spectra of the GQDs/TiO2 NFs and TiO2 NFs.
XRD patterns of the FTO and rutile type TiO2 NFs grown on the FTO substrate.

(a) Schematic illustration of H2 and O2 generation under illumination, (b) CV measurements for determination of HOMO and LUMO energy levels and (c) charge transfer mechanism at GQDs/TiO2 NFs interface.

(a) J–V curves under illumination versus Ag/AgCl as reference and inset shows the Nyquist plots of bare TiO2 NFs and GQDs-15/TiO2 NFs photoanodes, (b) J–t curves at an applied potential of 0.5 V, (c) UV–vis and (d) Mott–Schottky spectra of the GQDs-15/TiO2 NFs and TiO2 NFs photoanodes.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots decorated rutile TiO2 nanoflowers for water splitting application

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of physics, Faculty of Basic Science, Tarbiat Modares University, P.O. Box: 14115-111, Tehran, Islamic Republic of Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of physics, Faculty of Basic Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Flower like rutile TiO2 films were decorated with green-photoluminescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and photovoltaic properties were investigated for water splitting application. Rutile TiO2 nanoflowers (NFs) and GQDs (average width of ∼12 nm) synthesized separately using a hydrothermal method and TiO2 NFs were decorated with various amounts of GQDs solution (x = 5, 10, 15 and 20 µL) by spin coating. Optical characterization reveals that GQDs are highly luminescent and absorb UV and visible light photons with wavelengths up to 700 nm. GQDs-x/TiO2 electrode shows a photocurrent enhancement of ∼ 95% compared to pristine TiO2 NFs for the optimum sample (x = 15 µL) at an applied potential of P = 0 V using 1 M Na2SO4 solution as electrolyte.

Under illumination, Graphene quantum dots decorated TiO2 photoanode absorbs the sunlight and leads to decomposition of water molecules to form O2 and H+ for water splitting application.
Image, graphical abstract

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen as a candidate alternative to the traditional fossil energy has become the focus of the global attention. Photoelectrochemical hydrogen generation with semiconductor materials constitutes the most attractive approach toward the direct conversion of solar energy [1–7]. TiO2 has been intensively studied as a photocatalyst to date due to its favorable band-edge positions, high resistance to photocorrosion, excellent physical and chemical stability, nontoxicity, low-cost and abundance [8,9]. However, TiO2 has some drawbacks such as a large band gap (3.2 eV for anatase and 3 eV for rutile phase) and a negligible light harvesting efficiency in visible light, which limit its quantum efficiency.

Various strategies have been investigated to attack these problems. Doping with interstitial atoms like N [10], S [11], doping with metal or non-metal elements and hydrogen annealing [12] have provided limited success in extending the absorption range of TiO2. Another common approach relies on sensitization with narrow bandgap semiconductors and generally entails the use of chalcogenide materials (CdS, CdSe, PbS, etc.) which pose environmental problems and exhibit fast photocorrosion in aqueous solutions [13,14].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) due to their unique properties, such as ultraviolet and blue to green luminescence, excellent photostability, biocompatibility, chemical inertness, environmentally benign, cost effective preparation and appropriate band positions against TiO2 band positions (CV and VB bands) are a good candidate for improving disadvantages of TiO2 and reducing the photoelectrons’ loss [15–21]. GQDs show great promise in a wide range of applications such as photocatalysis [22,23], sensing [24,25], light emitting diodes (LEDs) [26], and energy conversion or energy storage devices [27–29]. So far, various methods have been utilized to prepare GQDs, including cage-opening fullerenes [10], mechanical grinding exfoliation [11], rapid thermal annealing [30], electrochemical approach [5], electron-beam lithography [12], chemical oxidation of CX-72 carbon black [13], cutting large graphene oxide sheets via a hydrothermal route [14] and carbonizing suitable organic precursors [15]. However, most of the methods mentioned above suffer from some disadvantages such as difficulty to obtain starting materials, difficulty for functionalization, expensive, low yield and poor optoelectrical properties. For example, methods of the cage-opening fullerenes on the ruthenium surfaces and carving graphite crystallites by high-resolution electron-beam lithography are limited by the low productivity and high cost. Theoretical calculations revealed that interfacial charge transfer can mediate the direct excitation of electrons from GQD into TiO2 conduction band under visible-light irradiation and electron transfer takes place from GQD to TiO2 surface in both the physisorption and chemisorption cases [31], leading to hole accumulation that is necessary to water splitting process.

Here we have used rutile TiO2 nanoflowers (NFs) as a base semiconductor substrate for GQDs because of its large surface area originating from its shape and favorable band-edge positions and a low cost, high yield, one-step facile process method was used for preparing GQDs from only glucose powder as precursor in DI water and the obtained GQDs was utilized to sensitize TiO2 NFs. Explaining the electron transfer mechanism in GQDs/TiO2 interface and generation of H2 and O2 under photoirradiation in a photoelectrochemical cell, could be informative to promote the utilization of GQDs in photovoltaic systems.

TiO2 NFs were grown on the fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO, F:SnO2) using a hydrothermal method without TiO2 seed layer. In a typical synthesis, 12 mL of deionized water was mixed with 6 mL of concentrated hydrochloric acid to reach a total volume of 18 mL in a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave (75 mL volume). After addition of 0.6 mL of Titanium Isopropoxide (TTIP), the mixture was stirred under ambient conditions for 5 min. After that, the pieces of FTO substrates (F:SnO2, 1 × 1.5 cm2) were ultrasonically cleaned for 15 min in deionized water and in acetone and put into the autoclave. The hydrothermal synthesis was conducted at 160 °C for 10 h in an oven. After synthesis, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature naturally. The obtained samples were washed with deionized water and dried at 80 °C in air.

The GQDs were prepared by a hydrothermal method using glucose and deionized water as precursors. In a typical synthesis, 0.05 g of glucose was dissolved in 50 mL of deionized water. The mixture was then treated by a one-step hydrothermal method in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave (75 mL) at 200 °C for 12 h, producing GQDs’ dispersions with a good solubility in water after filtration with the qualitative filter paper. The initial liquid sample was transparent (colorless) and it changed to orange after synthesis of GQDs. Under the hydrothermal process, glucose molecules are dehydrated to form C=C, which is the elementary unit of GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Decorating of TiO2 NFs with GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Herein, GQDs were used for sensitizing of TiO2 NFs by spin coating method. Various amounts of GQDs solution (x = 5, 10, 15 and 20 µL) was dropped on TiO2 NFs substrates under rotation speed and deposition time of 3000 rpm and 3 min, respectively. Then to better tenacity of GQDs on the TiO2 NFs surfaces, the GQDs-x/TiO2 NF photoanodes were heated for 30 min in an oven at 200 °C.

Photoluminescence (PL) emission was recorded using PL spectroscopy (Gilden photonics) and optical absorbance measurements were carried out using a spectrophotometer (Unico 4802). Structural morphology of samples was studied using a scanning electron microscope and transmission electron microscope (CM30 300 kV). Steady state current density voltage (J–V) and EIS measurements were carried out using a VSP-300 Multichannel Potentiostat/Galvanostat EIS (Bio-Logic Science Instruments). Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was employed using a FTIR spectrometer (Nicolet Nexus 670). Raman analysis (BRUKER, Germany, SENTERRA) was used for proving of formation of rutile phase TiO2 and appearance of GQDs on the TiO2 NFs surfaces. For IV and EIS measurement, three electrode configurations was used, where the TiO2 NFs and GQDs-x/TiO2 NFs photo-electrode were connected to the working electrode, and a saturated Ag/AgCl was used as the reference electrode and a Pt wire was connected to the counter electrode. The solution of Na2SO4 (1 M, pH 6) was used as the electrolyte, and electrodes were illuminated using a xenon lamp and the light intensity was adjusted with a thermopile to 100 mW/cm2. CV measurements were performed with a three electrode configuration, i.e. GQDs/glassy carbon as the working electrode, Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode and a platinum wire as the counter electrode in an acetonitrile solution including 0.1 M tetrabutylammonium hexafluorophosphate (TBAPF6).

Fig. 1 (a) and (b) shows typical top view SEM images of the TiO2 NFs films grown at 160 °C for 10 h in two different scales. SEM images reveal that the TiO2 NFs are perpendicular to the FTO substrate and the entire surface of the FTO substrate is covered uniformly with TiO2 NFs. Inset in Fig. 1(a) shows a cross section SEM image of the TiO2 NFs film. According to this picture, TiO2 NFs are composed from nanorods, with an average diameter determined about 150 nm with a thickness of about 4 µm. Fig. 1(c) shows TEM image of the obtained GQDs, which can be observed to have a uniform dispersion without apparent aggregation with the average size of about 12 nm, and the GQDs decorated TiO2 NFs are shown in Fig. 1(d).

UV–vis absorption spectra of the glucose and GQDs solutions are shown in Fig. 2 (a). Glucose solution has a peak around ∼200 nm and primary peak at 230 nm was assigned to π–π* transitions of aromatic CC bonds in sp 2 hybridization region and peak at about 300 nm is attributed to the n–π* transition of CO bonds [19,32,33]. Thus, the absorption spectrum could also provide the fingerprints of GQD structures. According to this figure, absorption region is recorded at all wavelengths in the spectrum and a strong optical absorption appeared near UV region with a tail extending in the visible range. Absorption at visible regions originates from connected functional groups. Such wide absorption allows excitation with a spectrally wide light source, necessary to perform efficiently in photovoltaic devices. GQDs possess defect, oxygen groups and functional groups on the edge and surface and because of their confined size will show quantum confinement effects. As a result, GQDs have a non-zero bandgap and PL on excitation.

Because the absorption peak of glucose solution is about 200 nm, glucose solution does not have any emission at Vis range. For GQDs solution, photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured using a spectrofluorophotometer equipped with a xenon lamp and the GQDs solution was excited at wavelengths from 450 to 520 nm with an interval of 10 nm. The corresponding PL spectra are shown in the inset of Fig. 2(a). The emission peaks of the sample at various excitation wavelengths did not shift and the maximum emission wavelength remained at around 540 nm that proves the obtained GQDs is green photoluminescent, which implies that both the size and the surface state of the sample remain uniform.

FTIR spectroscopy was applied to characterize the functional groups of the prepared GQDs. A possible formation mechanism of highly luminescent GQDs is proposed based on Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) analyses. As can be seen in Fig. 2(b), the following peaks are observed. Hydroxyl groups include broad peak at 3400 cm−1 related to OH stretching vibration and 1290 cm−1 OH bending vibrations in COH, respectively, and this functional group improves the hydrophilicity and stability of GQDs in aqueous systems. Peaks at 1620 cm−1 related to CC stretching vibrations, 1730 cm−1 to the stretching vibration of CO and 1380 cm−1 to the symmetric stretching vibration of carboxylic group –COO. All peaks in this figure are in agreement with peaks of GQDs [34].

Raman spectroscopy was employed to confirm the formation of TiO2 rutile phase and the existence of GQDs on the TiO2 NFs surfaces. Raman spectra of TiO2 NFs and GQDs/TiO2 NFs are shown in Fig 2(c). The spectrum of TiO2 NFs reveals two Raman peaks at 450 and 620 cm−1, which were assigned to E g and A1g modes [35], respectively. These peaks could be well attributed to the rutile phase of TiO2. These results from Raman spectroscopy led to the conclusion that rutile TiO2 NFs were grown on the FTO substrate. Two additional peaks at about 1354 and 1600 cm−1 for graphene structure are observed in the Raman spectrum for the GQDs/TiO2 NFs, which can be assigned to the disordered sp 2 carbon (D-band) and well-ordered graphite (G-band) [36,37], respectively. The intensity ratio of D to G bands indicates a disorder in graphene, which originates from defects associated with grain boundaries, vacancies and amorphous carbons. This confirms the significant growth and incorporation of GQDs on the surfaces of TiO2 NFs.

Fig. 3 shows the XRD patterns of FTO and TiO2 NFs grown on the FTO substrate. The relation between the FTO substrate and rutile TiO2 with a small lattice mismatch plays a key role in driving the nucleation and growth of the rutile TiO2 NFs on the FTO as substrate. According to this figure, the rutile TiO2 film deposited on FTO substrate and all diffraction peaks agree well with the tetragonal rutile phase [38,39]. No peaks of anatase or brookite phase were detected, indicating the purity of the rutile phase. As can be seen in Fig. 3, other peaks originate from the FTO substrate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Generation mechanism of H2 and O2 molecules
ce:section.ce:para: Electron–hole generation at GQDs, transmission of electrons to TiO2 NFs and generation of O2 and H2 are schematically explained in Fig. 4 (a). To generate H2 and O2 molecules, the position of conduction and valance bands of the semiconductor should fit into the potential window of water reduction and oxidation potential [40], respectively. Both TiO2 and GQDs have convenient position of conduction and valance bands (CB and VB) for water splitting [41]. When light shines on the GQDs-x/TiO2 NFs photoanode, GQDs absorb photons then electrons in the VB of GQDs are elevated to the CB of GQDs leaving holes behind. The excited electrons move to CB of TiO2 NFs and then gathered using charge collector and transport to counter electrode with an external contact, leading to generation of H2 on the counter electrode (Pt wire). Simultaneously, according to band positions, holes are injected into solution and oxidize the H2O molecules leading to formation of H+ and OH. Again, holes oxidize OH leading to formation of O2 on the photoanode electrode and Protons (H+) move to counter electrode through the electrolyte.

Highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of the GQDs against vacuum determined using a cyclic voltammetry technique. Cyclic voltammetry measurements were utilized to directly determine the LUMO and HOMO energy levels of the GQDs, and hence to find the bandgap energy. Applying potentials above the LUMO to form an accumulation layer, or below the HOMO to form inversion layers, can lead to abrupt emergence of cathodic and anodic currents, respectively [30–33]. Electrode for electrochemical analysis was prepared by drop-casting GQDs suspensions (5 µL) and nafion (0.5 µL, 5% w) onto a glassy carbon electrode (diameter of 3 mm) and dried naturally at room temperature. Fig. 4-b shows the behavior of the glassy carbon electrode in the absence and presence of the GQDs. The oxidation and reduction peaks related to electron and hole injections into the conduction and valence bands were consequently utilized to determine the E HOMO and E LUMO using the following three equations [42]
E HOMO
= − e
(
E
Ox , versus
NHE
+ 4.75 )
( eV )
E LUMO
= − e
(
E Red,
versus NHE
+ 4.75 )
( eV )
E NHE
=
E
Ag / AgCl
+ 0.197

The GQDs/glassy carbon electrode shows a reduction peak at −0.65 V and an oxidation peak at +2.15 V versus Ag/AgCl. The values for E LUMO and E HOMO calculated from these peaks are E LUMO = −4.3 eV and E HOMO = −7.1 eV versus vacuum, respectively. The charge transfer in the GQDs/TiO2 NFs interface can be understood by the energy diagram showed in Fig. 4(c), where the conduction band position of GQDs is higher than that of TiO2.

Fig. 5 (a) shows the measured photocurrent densities for GQDs-x/TiO2 NFs photoanodes as a function of applied bias voltages ranging from −0.4 to 0.9 V, in which x = 5, 10, 15 and 20 µL with concentration of 50 g/L. As well as Fig 5(b) shows the measured photocurrent densities for GQDs-15/TiO2 and pristine TiO2 at a constant potential of 0.5 V versus Ag/AgCl as reference. The charge transfer kinetics is systematically faster for GQDs-x/TiO2 NFs photoanodes against pristine TiO2 NFs as evidenced by the higher charge versus Ag/AgCl as reference. All of the GQD-x/TiO2 photoanodes exhibit a higher photocurrent density than the bar TiO2 NFs. Under illumination, the resulting photoinduced charge accumulation at GQDs is believed to be the reason for the significantly increased current density. The possible mechanism of the observed photoinduced charge accumulation at GQDs/TiO2 NFs can be explained because the GQDs can be chemisorbed onto TiO2 NFs surface through the OTi covalent bonds and positive work function difference [31], which is able to drive electron transfer from GQDs into TiO2, leading to charge separation across the GQDs/TiO2 NFs interface. The photocurrent generation of the GQD-x/TiO2 NFs photoanodes increases along with the increase in amount of added GQDs from 5 to 15 µL. Further increasing in x from 15 to 20 leads to less effective connection between surface excited electrons and electron–hole recombination is more probable due to slowing down the electron injection process, leading to decrease in photocurrent density of the GQD-x/TiO2 NFs photoanodes. In other words, when the TiO2 NFs are covered with excess GQDs, the effective surface area participating in reduction/oxidation processes is reduced and the probability of the photo-generated e––h+ recombination is increased resulting in lower J ph value. A similar behavior has been also seen for the CdS/CdSe [43] and Fe2O3/TiO2 nanotube [44] systems. These two positive and negative sides of addition of GQDs resulted in an optimum amount of x = 15 µL leading to the highest current density.

For further study on the charge transfer between GQDs and TiO2 NFs, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were performed. Typical EIS measurements for bare TiO2 NFs and optimum photoanode, GQDs-x/TiO2 NFs (x = 15 µL), were presented as Nyquist plots under illumination, where the horizontal axis is the real part (Z 1) and the vertical axis is the imaginary part (Z 2) of the samples’ impedance, as shown in the inset of Fig. 5. A semicircle with a near-zero intercept in high frequencies and a shorter tail in low frequencies were observed for the applied voltage of P = 0 V versus Ag/AgCl. Under illumination, semi-circle of the GQDs-15/TiO2 NFs photoanode is smaller than that of the bare TiO2 NFs photoanode indicating a lower charge transfer resistance in the GQDs-15/TiO2 NFs. R ct (charge transfer resistance) for bare TiO2 NFs photoanode was obtained 1410 Ω under illumination which is larger than 650 Ω for the GQDs-15/TiO2 NFs photoanode. It is well known that the lower charge transfer resistance is in favor of higher photo-current production, which is in a good agreement with J–V curves described above. As it is discussed above, photoexcited electrons generated at GQDs enhance the charge density.

To obtain more information about mechanism of increased photocurrent, UV–vis and Mott–Schottky plots for the GQDs-15/TiO2 and TiO2 NFs photoanodes were prepared. Fig. 5(c) shows UV–vis absorption spectra for both photoanodes. As can be seen, GQDs-15/TiO2 photoanode has a better absorption in the visible region versus the bare TiO2 and this absorption is a reason for enhancement in the generated photocurrent. The carrier densities of GQDs-15/TiO2 NFs and pristine TiO2 NFs can be compared from the slopes of Mott–Schottky plots using the following equation [45].
N d
=
2
e 0
ɛ
ɛ
0
[
d
(
1
C 2
)
d V
]
− 1
where N d is the charge carrier density, e 0 is the electron charge, ε is the dielectric constant of rutile TiO2 (ε = 87) [45], and ε 0 is the permittivity of vacuum. Mott–Schottky plot is shown in Fig. 5(d). Based on Mott–Schottky plots, the GQDs-15/TiO2 sample show smaller slope than that of the pristine TiO2 NFs, suggesting a significantly increase of charge carrier density.

In conclusion, we have prepared GQDs as effective sensitizers for rutile TiO2 NFs for enhancing the light harvesting efficiency and the catalytic activity for water oxidation. GQDs due to their enhanced properties improve drawbacks of pristine TiO2 electrodes. The photocurrent generated in the GQDs decorated TiO2 NFs system results from the transfer of photoexcited electrons in GQDs to the conduction band of TiO2 NFs and may serve as an efficient application in photoelectrochemical systems.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank Research Council of the Tarbiat Modares University for financial supports.
